mula_devanagari: कुतस् त्वा कश्मलम् इदं विषमे समुपस्थितम् | अनार्य-जुष्टम् अस्वर्ग्यम् अकीर्ति-करम् अर्जुन
mula_transliteration: kutas tvā kaśmalam idaṃ viṣame samupasthitam | anārya-juṣṭam asvargyam akīrti-karam arjuna

advaita.bhashya_note: Śaṅkarācārya does not comment on BG 2.2; his bhāṣya opens at 2.10. No rendering anchored to supplied bhāṣya prose is possible. The verse stands in the Advaita panel as un-glossed by Śaṅkara himself.
advaita.rendering: Where has this defilement (kaśmala) come from to seize you here, at this moment of crisis (viṣama)? It is not the behavior of one who knows what is real, it does not lead toward the higher good, and it destroys the only thing a kṣatriya can leave behind. [Note: Śaṅkarācārya's bhāṣya is absent for this verse; this rendering follows the logical structure of 2.10 onward, where he opens by identifying grief as rooted in the confusion between ātman and body — the kaśmala is thus the symptom of avidyā (ignorance of one's true nature) before the diagnosis is named.]
advaita.anchor: Śaṅkara bhāṣya absent for 2.2; logical inference from 2.10 context only

viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_note: Anchored to Rāmānuja's prose: 'avidvat-sevita' (practiced only by the unwise), 'paraloka-virodhī' (opposed to the other world), 'atikṣudra' (utterly petty), 'hṛdaya-daurbalya-kṛta' (produced by weakness of heart).
viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Having seen Arjuna seated in paralysis, the Lord immediately challenges him: whence has this grief (śoka) arisen in you, here in the midst of this crisis — grief that is practiced only by those without wisdom (avidvat-sevita), that runs counter to your welfare in the world beyond (paraloka-virodhī), and that is, at its root, nothing more than the product of a heart gone weak (hṛdaya-daurbalya-kṛta)? Abandon this utterly petty (atikṣudra) thing and rise to fight — for Bhagavān's command is itself the call that restores right relation.
viśiṣṭādvaita.anchor: Rāmānuja: 'avidvat-sevitaṃ paraloka-virodhina-akīrtikaram atikṣudraṃ hṛdaya-daurbalya-kṛtaṃ parityajya yuddhāya uttiṣṭha'

dvaita.bhashya_note: Madhvācārya does not comment on BG 2.2; his bhāṣya opens at 2.11. No rendering anchored to supplied bhāṣya prose is possible.
dvaita.rendering: Whence has this impurity (kaśmala) seized you, Arjuna, at this critical juncture (viṣama)? It is not the way of those who stand correctly in relation to Hari; it obstructs the dharma that leads beyond, and it ruins the only standing a warrior earns before others. [Note: Madhvācārya's bhāṣya is absent for this verse; this rendering follows Dvaita structural logic wherein the jīva's collapse into grief is a failure of dependent relationship with the Lord, not a mere emotional event — the Dvaita gloss on kaśmala would identify it as the jīva forgetting its ontological subordination to Viṣṇu.]
dvaita.anchor: Madhva bhāṣya absent for 2.2; logical inference from 2.11 context only

śuddhādvaita.bhashya_note: Anchored to Vallabha's prose: Arjuna addressed as 'śuddha-svarūpa' (pure in essence); 'kaśmala' in 'viṣame saṅkaṭe' (a moment of danger); 'moha-madhuhantā' (Kṛṣṇa as slayer of the intoxication of delusion) frames the entire utterance.
śuddhādvaita.rendering: O Arjuna, you whose essential nature is pure (śuddha-svarūpa) — how has this defilement (kaśmala) even arrived? You stand in the midst of danger (viṣame saṅkaṭe), yet the impurity is not outside you; it is the delusion that has moved into the space where Kṛṣṇa's prasāda (grace-overflow) should be. The slayer of delusion's intoxication (moha-madhuhantā) now speaks precisely to dissolve what should never have been yours.
śuddhādvaita.anchor: Vallabha: 'śuddhasvarūpa kuta idaṃ ca kaśmalaṃ samupasthitam' — Arjuna as pure-by-nature, kaśmala as intrusion

bhakti.bhashya_note: Anchored to Śrīdhara's four-part gloss: 'kaśmala' = moha (delusion/spiritual filth); 'āryair asevitam' (not practiced by the noble); 'asvargyam adharmyam' (against dharma, against heaven); 'ayaśaskara' (fame-destroying).
bhakti.rendering: Kṛṣṇa asks: for what reason (kuto hetoḥ) has this kaśmala — this spiritual filth, this fog of delusion (moha) — come over you in a moment of crisis? It is a thing the noble (āryāḥ) do not practice; it stands against right action and the good that follows from it (asvargyam adharmyam); and it will strip away the only renown (yaśas) you have built.
bhakti.anchor: Śrīdhara: 'kuta iti — āryair asevitam, asvargyam adharmyam ayaśaskaram ca'

advaita-bhakti.bhashya_note: Anchored to Madhusūdana's elaborate prose: Bhagavān defined by six complete qualities (ṣaḍ-guṇa: aiśvarya, dharma, yaśas, śrī, vairāgya, mokṣa); kaśmala as 'śiṣṭa-garhita' (censured by the learned); triple-fault structure — not serving mokṣa (āryāḥ = mumukṣavaḥ), not serving svarga (asvargyam), not serving kīrti (akīrti-karam); Arjuna addressed implicitly as 'sarva-kṣatriya-pravara' (foremost among kṣatriyas).
advaita-bhakti.rendering: He who is called Bhagavān is so named precisely because he holds, undivided and without obstruction, all six fullnesses — lordship, right action, renown, beauty, detachment, and liberation (aiśvarya, dharma, yaśas, śrī, vairāgya, mokṣa) — and such a one now turns to Arjuna, foremost among all kṣatriyas (sarva-kṣatriya-pravara), and asks: how has this kaśmala, this thing censured by every learned person (śiṣṭa-garhita), arrived in you? It serves neither those who seek liberation (mumukṣu), for the noble do not practice it; nor those who seek the higher worlds, for it opposes the dharma that leads there; nor those who seek glory, for it produces only its opposite — and therefore it is, by triple proof, indefensible.
advaita-bhakti.anchor: Madhusūdana: 'śiṣṭa-garhitatvena malinaṃ... āryair mumukṣubhiḥ... asvargyaṃ... akīrtikaram... hetutraya-niṣedha'

so_what_questions.question: I'm a founder mid-crisis — my co-founder just quit, funding is uncertain, the team is watching me. I feel frozen. Is there a difference between legitimate grief and the kind of collapse this verse is diagnosing?
The verse says this behavior is 'not practiced by the noble' — but isn't it human to break down? Why is vulnerability being shamed here?
As a doctor I sometimes freeze before delivering a hard diagnosis to a patient's family. Is that the kaśmala being named, or is it appropriate hesitation?
I'm a parent watching my child fail and I want to step in and rescue them. Is that the kind of misplaced grief and confusion this verse is talking about?

The verse ties the problem to three separate failures — it doesn't serve your spiritual good, it doesn't serve your worldly reputation, and it doesn't serve heaven. Why is the argument structural rather than just emotional?
Why does it matter that Kṛṣṇa asks 'from where?' rather than just saying 'stop grieving'? Is the origin of a mental state the real issue?

I'm a manager who went soft in a difficult performance conversation to protect my own comfort, not the employee's good. Is that the same structure — noble-sounding collapse that actually serves no one?

advaita.application: When you freeze in a difficult moment — a hard conversation, a deadline, a confrontation you've been avoiding — the Advaita mechanism says: ask where the freeze is coming from. Not what triggered it, but what is doing the freezing. If the answer is 'I', trace that 'I' — it is a constructed story about yourself that is being threatened, not the actual you. The kaśmala (spiritual filth) is the mistaking of that story for your true nature. Act from what remains when the story is set aside.
advaita.anchor: Logical extension of Śaṅkara's 2.10 opening: grief = avidyā of ātman/body distinction

viśiṣṭādvaita.application: Rāmānuja's gloss names the collapse as 'hṛdaya-daurbalya' (weakness of heart) — not an emotion but a structural failure of relationship. In practice: when you are incapacitated by what someone else might think, or what might be lost, ask whether your action is oriented toward the person you are actually serving (a patient, a child, your team) or toward your own comfort. Grief oriented around your image is 'paraloka-virodhī' — it runs against the actual good you are here to produce. Reorient the action as service, and the weakness dissolves into function.

dvaita.application: Madhva's tradition insists that the jīva (individual self) is constitutively dependent on and distinct from the Lord — collapse happens when you act as though the outcome is yours to control. In a professional crisis, this looks like: taking a loss personally in a way that paralyzes the next decision. The Dvaita correction is not detachment from outcome but correct placement of agency — you execute the role fully, the result belongs to Hari. The kaśmala is the illusion that your grief has any ownership over what happens next.
dvaita.anchor: Logical extension of Madhva's 2.11 framing of jīva as paratantra (dependent)

śuddhādvaita.application: Vallabha addresses Arjuna as 'śuddha-svarūpa' — pure in nature — meaning the defilement is not constitutive, it is a temporary occupation of space that belongs to Kṛṣṇa's grace (prasāda). In everyday terms: you are not broken, you have been interrupted. The Puṣṭi-mārga (path of nourishment) practice is to return your attention to the source of nourishment rather than to the disruption. When you are flooded by panic or grief, the move is not discipline but return — re-orient toward what feeds you, and the kaśmala clears on its own.
śuddhādvaita.anchor: Vallabha: 'śuddhasvarūpa kuta idaṃ ca kaśmalaṃ samupasthitam'

bhakti.application: Śrīdhara's four-fold diagnosis gives a practical checklist: is what I am doing right now something that (a) a person I respect would do, (b) produces a good I actually want in my life, (c) leads toward the better version of this situation, and (d) builds rather than destroys what I want to be known for? If no to all four, the behavior is kaśmala — not a moral failure but a functional one. The correction is not self-flagellation but the same question Kṛṣṇa asks: where did this come from, and does it belong here?

advaita-bhakti.application: Madhusūdana's triple-fault structure (fails the mokṣa-seeker, fails the svarga-seeker, fails the kīrti-seeker) means your paralysis is indefensible from any goal you might actually hold. This is the synthesizing move: you don't need a philosophical position to see that the collapse doesn't serve. In a workplace context — a manager going soft in a performance review, a teacher passing a student who hasn't earned it — the 'kindness' that is actually avoidance fails every standard simultaneously: it doesn't serve the person's growth, it doesn't serve the team's functioning, and it doesn't build the leader's credibility. Madhusūdana's point is that the excuse collapses under its own weight.
advaita-bhakti.anchor: Madhusūdana: 'hetutraya-niṣedha' — mokṣa/svarga/kīrti all three refuted by the same act

self_assessment.doctrinal_divergence_score: high — Vallabha's śuddha-svarūpa address, Rāmānuja's hṛdaya-daurbalya mechanism, Madhusūdana's ṣaḍ-guṇa framing, and Śrīdhara's four-fold checklist each deploy a genuinely distinct doctrinal mechanism; not merely tone-variation
self_assessment.bhashya_anchor_fidelity: high for viśiṣṭādvaita, śuddhādvaita, bhakti, advaita-bhakti — direct Sanskrit terms lifted from supplied prose; logical inference flagged and labeled for advaita and dvaita where bhāṣya is absent
self_assessment.absent_bhashya_handling: both absent cases (Śaṅkara, Madhva) are explicitly flagged in bhashya_note and anchor fields; renderings labeled as logical inference, not fabricated commentary
self_assessment.so_what_question_specificity: high — all seven questions are verse-specific (kaśmala at crisis, noble/vulnerable distinction, origin question, triple-fault structure); none are generic Gītā questions
self_assessment.everyday_application_distinctiveness: high — Advaita traces the 'I', Viśiṣṭādvaita redirects toward the served other, Dvaita places agency in the Lord, Śuddhādvaita names return to prasāda, Bhakti gives a four-question checklist, Advaita-bhakti deploys triple-fault as professional diagnostic; recognizably distinct without religious-gauze
self_assessment.known_limitation: Advaita and Dvaita renderings are logical inferences from adjacent bhāṣya material, not anchored in supplied text for this specific verse
